content: If $\sin x+\sin ^2 x=1$ then $\cos ^8 x+2 \cos ^6 x+\cos ^4 x$ is equal to :
explanation:
Let’s set $s=\sin x$. The equation

$$s+s^2=1$$

gives

$$s^2+s-1=0\;\Longrightarrow\;s=\frac{-1\pm\sqrt5}2.$$

Only $s=\frac{\sqrt5-1}2$ lies in $[-1,1]$.

Then

$$\cos^2x=1-s^2=1-\Bigl(\tfrac{\sqrt5-1}2\Bigr)^2=\frac{\sqrt5-1}2=s\,. $$

So $\cos^2x=s$, and

$ \cos^8x+2\cos^6x+\cos^4x =\cos^4x\bigl(\cos^4x+2\cos^2x+1\bigr) =\cos^4x\,(1+\cos^2x)^2. $

Replacing $\cos^2x=s$ gives

$ =s^2\,(1+s)^2. $

But from $s+s^2=1$ we have $s^2=1-s$. Hence

$ s^2\,(1+s)^2=(1-s)(1+s)^2 =(1-s)(1+2s+s^2) =(1-s)\bigl(2+s\bigr) =2-2s+s- s^2 =2 -s - (1-s) =1. $

Answer: $\displaystyle1$. (Option B)

content: If for real values of $x, \cos \theta=x+\frac{1}{x}$, then $X$
explanation:
Let’s look at the range of

$$f(x)=x+\frac1x\,,\;x\ne0.$$

For $x>0$, by AM–GM:

$$x+\frac1x\ge2\,,\quad\text{with equality at }x=1.$$

For $x<0$, set $y=-x>0$ gives

$$x+\frac1x=-\Bigl(y+\frac1y\Bigr)\le-2\,,\quad\text{with equality at }y=1\;(x=-1).$$

So

$$f(x)\in(-\infty,-2]\cup[2,\infty)\,. $$

But for a real angle $\theta$,

$$\cos\theta\in[-1,1]\,. $$

There is no overlap between $[-1,1]$ and $(-\infty,-2]\cup[2,\infty)$, so

no real $\theta$ can satisfy

$$\cos\theta=x+\frac1x\,. $$

Answer: Option B (no value of $\theta$ is possible).

content: The general solution of $$2 \cos 4 x+\sin ^2 2 x=0$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } 2 \cos 4 x+\sin ^2 2 x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 \cos 4 x+\left(\frac{1-\cos 4 x}{2}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 \cos 4 x+1=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \cos 4 x=-\frac{1}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & \sin 4 x= \pm \frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & 4 x=n \pi+(-1)^n \sin ^{-1}\left( \pm \frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & x=\frac{n \pi}{4}+\frac{(-1)^n}{4} \sin ^{-1}\left( \pm \frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}\right) \end{array}$$

$$\sin 2 x+\cos x=0$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & 2 \sin x \cos x+\cos x=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos x(2 \sin x+1)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos x=0 \text { or } 2 \sin x+1=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos x=\cos \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow x=n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{2}, n \in z \\ \text { or } & \sin x=-\frac{1}{2}=-\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \\ & =\sin \left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \sin x=\sin \left(\frac{7 \pi}{6}\right) \\ \therefore & x=n \pi+(-1)^n \frac{7 \pi}{6}, n \in Z \end{array} $$

content: If $$\sin 2x = 4\cos x$$, then x is equal to
explanation:
We have, $$\sin 2x = 4\cos x \Rightarrow 2\sin x\cos x = 4\cos x$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos (\sin x - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow \cos x = 0$$ [$$\because$$ $$\sin x \ne 2$$]

$$ \Rightarrow \cos x = 0 = \cos \pi /2 \Rightarrow x = 2n\pi \, \pm \,{\pi \over 2},n \in Z$$

content: If $$\sin 3\theta = \sin \theta $$, how many solutions exist such that $$ - 2\pi < \theta < 2\pi $$?
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & \sin 3 \theta=\sin \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^3 \theta=\sin \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 4 \sin ^3 \theta-2 \sin \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin \theta\left(2 \sin ^2 \theta-1\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta=0 \text { or } \sin \theta= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \text { If } \sin \theta=0 \text { and if } \sin \theta= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \theta=0, \pi,-\pi \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{-5 \pi}{4}, \frac{-7 \pi}{4}, \frac{-\pi}{4}, \frac{-3 \pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ There are nine solutions.
